title: Cibolo
name: Cibolo
metaDescription: 1031 exchange coordination for Cibolo industrial park, retail, and multifamily property, with Cibolo Creek floodplain review and current comparables upfront.

sections.html: Cibolo, part of the Schertz-Cibolo-Universal City growth corridor along I-35 and FM 78 in Guadalupe County, has added industrial parks, multifamily, and retail at a pace that has made it one of the fastest-growing cities in the San Antonio region. Investors using Cibolo as a 1031 replacement market are generally underwriting continued growth rather than existing stabilization, and documentation needs to reflect current, not historical, market conditions.

sections.heading: Cibolo Asset Stock and New Industrial Parks
sections.html:
New industrial parks along the FM 78 and I-35 corridors have added distribution and light-manufacturing space aimed at regional users who need proximity to both San Antonio and the broader Golden Triangle labor pool. Multifamily has expanded to house workers drawn by that growth, and retail has followed close behind the rooftops with grocery-anchored and service-retail centers.

Each of these classes satisfies the like-kind standard for real property held for investment or business use, so an investor exiting a different asset class elsewhere can identify Cibolo industrial or retail product without a classification issue.

Because much of Cibolo's industrial construction is recent, an exchange buyer often has more current building specifications and clear-height options to choose from than in older, more built-out San Antonio industrial submarkets, though that also means less operating history to underwrite against.

sections.heading: What Is Driving Growth in Cibolo
sections.html:
Cibolo's population growth rate has outpaced most of its neighbors in the Schertz-Cibolo-Universal City corridor, and new industrial park development has kept pace by adding distribution capacity rather than waiting for demand to fully materialize first. Land pricing has appreciated quickly as a result, which changes the math for exchange buyers accustomed to comparables from a year or two earlier.

Retail leasing has followed the same trajectory, with grocery and service tenants signing leases well ahead of a center reaching full residential build-out around it, a pattern that reflects confidence in the area's continued growth trajectory.

sections.heading: Closing Coordination for Cibolo Replacement Property
sections.html:
Because Cibolo Creek runs through several of the city's growth areas, floodplain confirmation is a standard early step before a parcel goes onto a written identification. Coordination for a Cibolo file typically includes:

  • Cibolo Creek floodplain and drainage easement confirmation for the specific parcel
  • Written identification meeting the unambiguous-description standard under Treasury Regulation 1.1031(k)-1
  • Current comparable sales given rapid recent price appreciation
  • Lender preflight sized to updated Cibolo land and building values
  • Exchange agreement and assignment documents delivered to the qualified intermediary before funding

This supports the file's documentation and timeline; it doesn't replace the investor's own engineering or market review.

sections.heading: Watchouts for Cibolo Investors
sections.html:
Rapid growth has, in places, run ahead of municipal service capacity, so a buyer underwriting near-term expansion should confirm water, sewer, and road capacity commitments directly with the city rather than relying on a general growth narrative. Cibolo Creek floodplain areas can also remove usable square footage from an otherwise attractive site, and fast price appreciation means older comparable sales data can overstate or understate current value depending on when it was pulled.

Because so much of the local building stock is new, an investor should also confirm certificate-of-occupancy and warranty status carefully, since punch-list items on recently delivered industrial or retail space can otherwise surface after closing. Builder warranty terms and any outstanding contractor liens should be reviewed alongside the title commitment before the exchange agreement is finalized.

sections.heading: Coordinating With a Qualified Intermediary on Cibolo Property
sections.html: The qualified intermediary's role, holding proceeds and preparing exchange documents, is the same for Cibolo as for any other submarket, but given how quickly comparable values have moved, an investor should have current appraisal or broker opinion data in hand before finalizing a written identification, since a stale number can create a debt-replacement or boot problem that surfaces only at closing. Keeping a second identified property in a more established Guadalupe County corridor is also a reasonable hedge given how fast Cibolo pricing has moved, and it gives the investor a fallback if the primary deal's financing terms shift before closing.

faqs.q: Why has Cibolo land pricing moved so quickly compared to nearby cities?
faqs.a: Rapid population and industrial park growth has pulled demand ahead of available supply in several Cibolo submarkets, which has pushed land values up faster than in some neighboring Guadalupe County cities.

faqs.q: Does a Cibolo industrial park building qualify as like-kind against multifamily I'm exchanging out of?
faqs.a: Yes, both are real property held for business or investment use, which satisfies the like-kind requirement regardless of asset class, though your tax advisor should still confirm the specific facts of your file.

faqs.q: How does Cibolo Creek floodplain exposure affect my identification?
faqs.a: Floodplain and drainage easement status should be confirmed before the parcel is named in your written identification, since it can reduce usable square footage or trigger additional site-plan requirements that a lender will want resolved before funding.

faqs.q: Should I get a new appraisal given how fast Cibolo values have changed?
faqs.a: Given the pace of recent appreciation, current comparable data is worth confirming rather than relying on numbers pulled even a few months earlier, particularly for lender preflight and boot calculations tied to your identified property.

faqs.q: Is Cibolo's growth outpacing city services?
faqs.a: In some areas, yes; confirming water, sewer, and road capacity commitments directly with the city is a standard step for anyone underwriting near-term expansion on a Cibolo property, and that confirmation should be dated close to the identification, not pulled from an earlier due-diligence cycle.
